params.description: Scale Preview System from Warmup appears. Student's data visible. All four scale options (1, 2, 5, 10) showing checkmarks.

beats.text: In Warmup, all four scales worked for your data. You had options!

elements.description: Scale Preview System from Warmup. Student's data with all four scales showing checkmarks.

beats.text: But what happens when the numbers are bigger? Let's find out.

elements.description: Scale Preview System from Warmup. Student's data with all four scales showing checkmarks.

params.description: Data table appears. Books Read This Month. Aisha: 20, Ben: 35, Carlos: 55, Dana: 80.
Vertical bar graph appears. Books Read This Month. Axis 0-50. Scale of 5 pre-selected. Graph initially empty.

beats.text: Here's data about how many books four students read this month. Dana read the most—80 books!

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values.
Vertical bar graph. Books Read This Month. Axis 0-50. Scale of 5 selected. Graph empty.

beats.text: Let me try a scale of 5 and see what happens.

params.description: Preview generates. Aisha (20), Ben (35), Carlos (55) bars appear normally. Dana's bar (80) extends past the graph boundary. Warning indicator appears.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values.
Vertical bar graph. Aisha, Ben, Carlos bars visible. Dana's bar extends past top. Warning indicator showing.

beats.text: Hmm. Carlos's bar is just above my graph, but Dana's bar goes past the top. The graph only goes up to 50 right now.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values.
Vertical bar graph. Aisha, Ben, Carlos bars visible. Dana's bar extends past top. Warning indicator showing.

beats.text: I could make the graph taller to fit 80. Let's see... I'd need 55, 60, 65, 70, 75, 80...

params.description: Axis extends upward. Tick marks appear: 55, 60, 65, 70, 75, 80. Axis now crowded with many tick marks.
Graph axis now extends to 80. All bars visible. Dana's bar reaches 80.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values.
Vertical bar graph. Axis 0-80 with 17 tick marks visible (0, 5, 10, 15... 75, 80). All bars visible. Dana's bar at 80.

beats.text: That's a lot of numbers on the axis. 0, 5, 10, 15, 20... all the way up to 80. That's 17 tick marks.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values.
Vertical bar graph. Axis 0-80. All 17 tick marks highlighted. All bars visible.

beats.text: It works, but is there a better way? When one scale needs too many tick marks, try a bigger scale.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values.
Vertical bar graph. Axis 0-80 with 17 tick marks. All bars visible.

incorrects.description: Student selected 20 instead of 80

params.description: 80 highlighted in the data table.

steps.text: 20 is in the data, but it's not the biggest. We need the largest value. Look at all the numbers. Which one is biggest?

incorrects.description: Student selected 35 instead of 80

params.description: 80 highlighted in the data table.

steps.text: 35 is in the data, but there's a bigger number. Look at all four values. Which one is the greatest?

incorrects.description: Student selected 55 instead of 80

params.description: 80 highlighted in the data table.

steps.text: 55 is close, but not the biggest. Compare all the numbers. Which value is larger than 55?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Values highlight in order: 20, 35, 55, 80. 80 remains highlighted as the largest.

steps.text: Let me show you. We compare all the values: 20, 35, 55, 80. The biggest number is 80. When choosing a scale, always check the biggest number first. That tells you what scales can fit all your data without going past the graph boundary.

incorrects.description: Student clicked a scale other than 1 on first attempt

steps.text: That one works too. Now click Scale of 1.

incorrects.description: Student clicked a scale other than 1 on second attempt

params.description: Scale of 1 button highlights.

steps.text: We want to see what Scale of 1 looks like. It's the button on the left. Click it.

incorrects.description: Student clicked a scale other than 1 three or more times. System models the action.

params.description: System clicks Scale of 1 button. Graph updates to show Scale of 1.

steps.text: Let me click Scale of 1 for us. The button on the left selects Scale of 1. When I click it, the graph updates to show what the data looks like with that scale. The axis now shows tick marks at every whole number from 0 to 23. That's 24 tick marks total. Scale of 1 shows every value exactly, but it creates a lot of marks on the axis for this data.

incorrects.description: Student clicked wrong scale button on first attempt

steps.text: Not quite. Look for the Scale of 2 button and click it.

incorrects.description: Student clicked wrong scale button on second attempt

params.description: Scale of 2 button highlights.

steps.text: Here's what to try: click the button that says 'Scale of 2'. It's part of the scale selector.

incorrects.description: Student clicked wrong scale button three or more times. System models the correct action.

params.description: System demonstrates: Scale transitions to 2. All bars land exactly on tick marks. Green checkmark indicator appears.

steps.text: Let me show you. I click the Scale of 2 button. The scale changes and now all the bars land exactly on tick marks. With Scale of 2, half of 2 is 1, so every whole number lands right on a line or exactly halfway between two lines.

incorrects.description: Student selected Data Set A (20, 35, 55, 80)

params.description: Data Set A highlights. Ones digits (0, 5, 5, 0) emphasized.

steps.text: Look at the ones place in Data Set A. Every number ends in 0 or 5. Those are multiples of 5.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Data Set B ones digits (7, 2, 9, 3) highlight in sequence.

steps.text: Let me show you. Look at Data Set B: 7, 12, 19, 23. The ones place shows 7, 2, 9, 3. None of those are 0 or 5, so these aren't multiples of 5. That pattern tells you Scale of 2 is the right tool. Data Set B is the answer.

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 1

params.description: Preview of Scale of 1 appears showing 32 tick marks from 0 to 31.

steps.text: Scale of 1 works for showing exact values. But count those marks: 32 lines to reach 31. That's a lot. Is there a cleaner way?

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 5

params.description: Values 22, 8, and 31 highlight in the data table.

steps.text: Look at 22, 8, 31. Their ones place digits are 2, 8, 1. None are 0 or 5, so they won't land exactly on Scale of 5. Which scale shows any whole number exactly?

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 10

params.description: Values 22, 8, and 31 highlight in the data table.

steps.text: Scale of 10 is clean, but look at 22, 8, 31. None end in 0 or 5, so they won't land exactly on the tick marks. We need a scale that shows any whole number exactly.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Bar graph with Scale of 2 appears. All four bars displayed at correct heights.
Each bar pulses to show it lands exactly on a line or halfway between lines. 22 is at 22 (on the line), 15 is halfway between 14 and 16, 8 is at 8 (on the line), 31 is halfway between 30 and 32.

steps.text: The answer is Scale of 2. Here's why: when values don't end in 0 or 5, Scale of 2 shows them exactly. Half of 2 is 1, so every whole number lands either on a line or exactly halfway between. 22 is on the line at 22. 15 is exactly halfway between 14 and 16. 8 is on the line at 8. 31 is exactly halfway between 30 and 32. Scale of 2 works for any whole number.

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 2

params.description: Dates value (60) highlighted in data table.

steps.text: Scale of 2 works, but look at the biggest number: 60. That means 30 tick marks on the axis. With Scale of 10, you only need 6 tick marks. Which is easier to read?

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 5

params.description: Scale of 5 and Scale of 10 options highlighted side by side.

steps.text: Scale of 5 fits all the data. But look: to reach 60, you need 12 tick marks. Scale of 10 only needs 6. Both work, but which graph is cleaner?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: System demonstrates Scale of 10. Axis shows 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60. All bars fit cleanly: Dates at 60, Cherries at 45, Bananas at 30, Apples at 15.

steps.text: Here's how I think about it. The biggest number is 60. All values end in 0 or 5, so Scale of 5 and Scale of 10 both show exact values. But Scale of 10 only needs 6 tick marks while Scale of 5 needs 12. Fewer marks make the graph easier to read. The answer is Scale of 10 because it balances precision with clarity.

not.selected: All values end in 0 or 5 so they can be graphed precisely.
The graph is easy to read.
The biggest number is 60 so the bars fit on the graph.
Scale of 2 takes too many marks to reach the biggest number.

incorrects.description: Branch 3: Student selected all incorrect answers, none correct

params.description: Data values highlighted with ones place digits shown: 60 (0), 45 (5), 30 (0), 15 (5).

steps.text: Look at what the question asks: why is Scale of 10 a good choice? Check the ones place digits. What do you notice about 60, 45, 30, 15?

or.selected: All values end in 0 or 5 so they can be graphed precisely.
The graph is easy to read.
The biggest number is 60 so the bars fit on the graph.
Scale of 2 takes too many marks to reach the biggest number.

and.selected: All values end in 0 or 5 so they can be graphed precisely.
The graph is easy to read.
The biggest number is 60 so the bars fit on the graph.
Scale of 2 takes too many marks to reach the biggest number.

not.selected: All values end in 0 or 5 so they can be graphed precisely.
The graph is easy to read.
The biggest number is 60 so the bars fit on the graph.
Scale of 2 takes too many marks to reach the biggest number.

incorrects.description: Branch 2: Student selected only correct answers, but not all of them

params.description: Scale preview system showing comparison: Scale of 2 (30 marks), Scale of 10 (6 marks).

steps.text: You found some correct reasons. Look at all four statements again. Each one explains something important about why Scale of 10 works well. Are there any others that also fit?

not.selected: All values end in 0 or 5 so they can be graphed precisely.
The graph is easy to read.
The biggest number is 60 so the bars fit on the graph.
Scale of 2 takes too many marks to reach the biggest number.

or.selected: All values end in 0 or 5 so they can be graphed precisely.
The graph is easy to read.
The biggest number is 60 so the bars fit on the graph.
Scale of 2 takes too many marks to reach the biggest number.

incorrects.description: Branch 4: Student selected mix of correct and incorrect answers

params.description: Correct selections highlighted in green.

steps.text: Some of your choices are right. But check the others carefully. Do they all explain why Scale of 10 is a good choice for this specific data?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: System highlights each reason in sequence: ones place digits showing 0 or 5, axis with only 6 clean tick marks, all bars fitting within range, comparison showing Scale of 2 would need 30 marks.

steps.text: Let me walk through why Scale of 10 works. First, all values end in 0 or 5, so they land exactly on tick marks or halfway between. Second, the graph is easy to read with only 6 marks on the axis. Third, the biggest number is 60, so all bars fit well. Fourth, Scale of 2 would need 30 marks to reach 60, which is too many. All four reasons explain why Scale of 10 is the best choice here.

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 10 for data with non-multiples of 5

params.description: Values 27, 33, and 41 highlight in the table.

steps.text: Look at 27, 33, and 41. Those numbers don't end in 0 or 5. Scale of 10 won't show them exactly. Which scale shows any whole number?

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 5 for data with non-multiples of 5

params.description: Values 27, 33, and 41 highlight in the table.

steps.text: Check those ones place digits: 7, 3, 1. None are 0 or 5, so Scale of 5 won't show them exactly. Which scale can show every whole number?

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 1 when the largest value is 41

params.description: Value 41 highlights in the table.

steps.text: Scale of 1 works, but count to 41. That's over 40 tick marks on the axis. For numbers this big, which scale shows them exactly without too many marks?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: System highlights ones place digits: 7, 4, 1, 3. None are 0 or 5.

steps.text: Let me show you. Look at the ones place: 7, 4, 1, 3. None are 0 or 5, so these aren't multiples of 5. Scale of 2 works because half of 2 is 1. That means we can show any whole number exactly. The answer is Scale of 2.

incorrects.description: Student set Guitar correctly but one or more other bars incorrectly

params.description: Guitar bar highlights at 27.

steps.text: Guitar is right at 27. Check the data table for the other instruments. Where should each bar end?

incorrects.description: Student set bar heights incorrectly on first attempt

steps.text: Check the data table. Look at the gridlines. Where should each bar end?

incorrects.description: Student set bar heights incorrectly on second attempt

params.description: Target heights appear as dashed lines. Gridlines highlight at each tick mark.

steps.text: With Scale of 2, each gridline is 2 apart. Guitar is 27, halfway between 26 and 28. Piano is 14, right on the line. Find where each value lands on the grid.

incorrects.description: Student set bar heights incorrectly three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System demonstrates placing each bar. Piano: 14, right on the line. Guitar: 27, halfway between 26 and 28. Drums: 33, halfway between 32 and 34. Violin: 41, halfway between 40 and 42.

steps.text: Let me show you. Piano is 14, right on a line. Guitar is 27, halfway between 26 and 28. Drums is 33, halfway between 32 and 34. Violin is 41, halfway between 40 and 42. With Scale of 2, half of 2 is 1, so every whole number lands exactly on a line or halfway between. The bars are: Guitar 27, Piano 14, Violin 41, Drums 33.

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 5 on first attempt

steps.text: Scale of 5 works for these numbers. But check if there's a bigger scale that's even easier to read.

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 2 on first attempt

steps.text: Scale of 2 fits. But 70 is a big number. Try a bigger scale to keep the axis cleaner.

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 1 on first attempt

steps.text: Not quite. Count to 70 by ones. That's a lot of marks on the axis. Try a bigger scale.

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 5 on second attempt

params.description: Data values highlighted showing ones place digits: 5, 5, 0, 0.

steps.text: Look at the last digits: all 0 or 5. These are multiples of 5. Both scales of 5 and 10 work. Which gives fewer marks on the axis to reach 70?

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 2 on second attempt

params.description: Data values highlighted showing ones place digits: 5, 5, 0, 0.

steps.text: Check the ones place: 5, 5, 0, 0. They're all 0 or 5, so these are multiples of 5. What's the biggest scale where all these numbers land exactly?

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 1 on second attempt

params.description: Largest value (70) highlighted.

steps.text: The biggest number is 70. Count by ones to 70: that's 70 tick marks. For bigger data, a bigger scale makes the graph easier to read. Which scale reaches 70 with the fewest marks?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System highlights ones place digits: 5, 5, 0, 0. Then shows comparison: Scale of 5 needs 14 marks (0, 5, 10...70), Scale of 10 needs 8 marks (0, 10, 20...70).

steps.text: Let me show you. All values end in 0 or 5, so both scales of 5 and 10 work. But look: Scale of 5 needs 14 tick marks to reach 70. Scale of 10 only needs 8. Same data, cleaner graph. For big data that's multiples of 5, Scale of 10 is best.

incorrects.description: Student set Red Team bar to 20 on first attempt

steps.text: Check the data table. How many points did Red Team score?

incorrects.description: Student set Red Team bar to 30 on first attempt

steps.text: Close. Red Team scored 25, not 30. Where does 25 land?

incorrects.description: Student set Red Team bar to any other wrong height on first attempt

steps.text: Not quite. Find 25 on the axis. It's halfway between two marks.

incorrects.description: Student set Red Team bar to 20 on second attempt

params.description: Red Team row highlighted showing value 25.

steps.text: Look at the data table. Red Team scored 25 points, not 20. Find the mark for 20, then go halfway to 30.

incorrects.description: Student set Red Team bar to 30 on second attempt

params.description: Axis marks at 20 and 30 highlighted.

steps.text: Red Team scored 25, not 30. Look at the axis marks: 20 and 30. Where does 25 go? Exactly halfway between those two marks.

incorrects.description: Student set Red Team bar to any other wrong height on second attempt

params.description: Axis marks at 20 and 30 highlighted with midpoint indicator.

steps.text: Red Team scored 25. The axis goes by tens: 0, 10, 20, 30. Find 20 and 30. 25 is exactly halfway between them. Place the bar there.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System demonstrates: highlights axis marks at 20 and 30, shows midpoint indicator at 25, then draws bar to that height. Bar locks at 25.

steps.text: Watch. Red Team scored 25. I find 20 and 30 on the axis. 25 is halfway between them. With Scale of 10, any number ending in 5 lands exactly at the midpoint. The bar for Red Team goes to 25.

incorrects.description: Student set Blue Team bar to 30 on first attempt

steps.text: Check the data table. How many points did Blue Team score?

incorrects.description: Student set Blue Team bar to 50 on first attempt

steps.text: Not quite. Blue Team scored 40, not 50. Find 40 on the axis.

incorrects.description: Student set Blue Team bar to any other wrong height on first attempt

steps.text: Look at the data table. Blue Team scored 40. That's right on a mark.

incorrects.description: Student set Blue Team bar to 30 on second attempt

params.description: Blue Team row highlighted showing value 40.

steps.text: Check the data table. Blue Team scored 40, not 30. Find the mark labeled 40 on the axis.

incorrects.description: Student set Blue Team bar to 50 on second attempt

params.description: Axis mark at 40 highlighted.

steps.text: Blue Team scored 40, not 50. The number ends in 0, so it lands exactly on a mark. Find the mark labeled 40.

incorrects.description: Student set Blue Team bar to any other wrong height on second attempt

params.description: Axis mark at 40 highlighted.

steps.text: Blue Team scored 40. That ends in 0, so it lands right on a mark. Look at the axis: 0, 10, 20, 30, 40. Place the bar at 40.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System demonstrates: highlights axis mark at 40, then draws bar directly to that mark. Bar locks at 40.

steps.text: Watch. Blue Team scored 40. The number ends in 0, so with Scale of 10, it lands exactly on a tick mark. I find 40 on the axis and place the bar right there. The bar for Blue Team goes to 40.

steps.text: Check the data table. How many points did Green Team score?

params.description: Vertical bar graph appears. Favorite Colors data from previous sections.

beats.text: You learned to choose scales. Check the biggest number. Does it fit without too many marks on the axis? Check the ones place digits. Are they 0 or 5 to find the exact place for the bar? For very small data, scale of 1 could work. Then pick the easiest to read.

elements.description: Vertical bar graph showing Favorite Colors data.

params.description: Bar graph clears from screen.
